lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced stevedoring, vessel loading and discharge, terminal transfer, container and break-bulk handling, bulk handling, Ro/Ro handling, and related on-terminal cargo movement to external carriers, terminal operators, shippers, and cargo owners.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive cargo-handling units serving only a parent carrier or cargo owner, port and pier operation classified in 488310, warehousing, freight packing, pure equipment leasing, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy labor- and equipment-intensive handling under per-unit, per-ton, per-container, gang-hour, time-and-materials, cost-plus, minimum-quantity, and broader terminal-service contracts, often within port concessions and coastwide labor frameworks.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures occupations across all NAICS 488300 water-transport support establishments, not 488320 alone, LMM firms, or task-level AI exposure. I map high exposure to billing, records, customer service, dispatch, planning, and clerical tasks; partial exposure to supervision, logistics, equipment routing, exception management, inspection, and safety documentation; and low direct substitution to crane, vehicle, vessel, rigging, and manual cargo work. Because 488320 likely has more material-moving labor than the broader group, this bridge may overstate exposure; because high-wage planning and supervisory tasks are only partially identified, it may understate wage-weighted exposure.
a.rationale: The broader 488300 workforce was 61.16% transportation and material moving, including 27.71% material movers and 17.10% hand laborers, while office and administrative support was 10.76%. GAO confirms process automation for tracking and communicating container movements at major ports and equipment automation at a minority of them. Current not-yet-automated opportunity is therefore concentrated in coordination, paperwork, planning, and machine-assistance rather than wholesale removal of cargo crews.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-and-time study across independent container, bulk, break-bulk, vehicle, and project-cargo stevedores, separated by union status and current automation, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Four-digit NAICS 488300 mixes marine cargo handling with port operations, pilots, tug services, and other water-support activities.
OEWS occupation shares are not task shares, and currently automated tasks are not separately identified.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis, while the occupation evidence is 2023.

rho.rationale: Within five years, operators can deploy AI for job intake, manifests and document extraction, gang and equipment planning, yard sequencing, gate support, maintenance prediction, safety reporting, billing, and customer updates. GAO found broad process automation but only four of the ten largest US container ports using automated cargo-handling equipment and reported mixed performance, cost, security, and labor effects. The ILA-USMX agreement covers East and Gulf Coast workers through September 2030, protects current jobs, and creates a negotiated framework for technology, materially constraining rapid labor substitution. LMM capital budgets and legacy integration add friction.
rho.change_evidence: Collect post-implementation results from independent stevedores and terminal-system vendors by workflow, plus side-letter and local labor-agreement provisions on technology, staffing, jurisdiction, retraining, and productivity through 2031.
caveats: The ILA-USMX agreement applies to Atlantic and Gulf Coast covered employers, not the West Coast or every inland and nonunion operation.
GAO's large-container-port sample may not transfer to smaller bulk, break-bulk, and project-cargo handlers.
The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a and excludes demand and pricing.

e.rationale: The six-digit definition is explicitly a service industry for stevedoring and marine cargo handling, so independent operators generally fit the outsourced lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive terminals owned by carriers or cargo interests, establishments embedded in large integrated terminal networks, project-only operators without repeat customer relationships, and entities whose economics depend mainly on real estate or warehousing. The frozen n of 40 estimated LMM-band firms is not re-estimated.
e.change_evidence: Classify the 40 estimated LMM-band firms using ownership, external-customer count, recurring and repeat revenue, cargo mix, union status, parent relationships, concession rights, and standalone financials.
caveats: NAICS is establishment-based while eligibility concerns transferable firms.
Integrated terminal operators can combine 488320 handling with 488310 port operation, warehousing, trucking, and shipping.
The frozen n is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a transportation-sector EBITDA margin and is quality ESTIMATE.

s5.bridge: The Census owner-age measure covers responding owners of employer businesses across industries, not LMM stevedores. Disclosed 2023 and 2026 transactions demonstrate transferability of US cargo-handling and terminal operations but provide no eligible-firm denominator and skew toward larger networks or assets. I translate broad succession pressure and active strategic consolidation into a five-year probability, discounting for corporate ownership, union and concession complexity, and internal succession. This may overstate transfer if approvals and capital needs deter buyers, or understate it if consolidation of specialized local operations accelerates.
s5.rationale: Generic Census data show 51% of responding employer-business owners were at least 55 in 2018. Pangaea acquired port terminal operations in Florida and Maryland in 2023; AMPORTS acquired a Texas stevedoring terminal in 2026; and Enstructure agreed to acquire LOGISTEC's North American marine-terminal division in 2026. These events show buyer appetite and operational transferability, but not an observed industry rate.
s5.change_evidence: Create a 2019-2026 census of stevedore company, business-line, and terminal-operation transfers matched to the verified eligible denominator, then interview owners and port counterparties on succession, assignment rights, and expected sale timing.
caveats: The owner-age source is cross-industry, old, and owner-level rather than firm-level.
Public deal announcements omit failed sales, closures, and internal reorganizations and skew large.
Terminal asset acquisitions and operating-right transfers are not always equivalent to buying a standalone LMM firm.

q.rationale: Per-unit, gang-hour, time-and-materials, and cost-plus pricing makes direct labor productivity visible and gives carriers and terminal customers leverage to demand lower rates or staffing at renewal. A public operator's filing says stevedoring consideration is based on a rate per unit, may include minimum quantities, and is allocated to obligations using expected cost plus margin. Some gains can still be retained during contract terms or monetized through higher throughput, faster turns, safety, and equipment uptime, especially where capacity or specialist labor is scarce.
q.change_evidence: Review 30 contracts and renewal outcomes across cargo types and ports, recording rate basis, labor and benefit pass-through, minimum quantities, escalation, productivity sharing, detention and turnaround incentives, customer concentration, and realized gross-margin change after workflow improvements.
caveats: One public-company accounting policy does not establish the mix of contract types among LMM stevedores.
Union wage increases and work rules may convert automation gains into negotiated labor or benefit costs.
Retention differs between dedicated terminals, spot stevedoring, government work, and integrated logistics contracts and excludes demand and implementation effects.

d5.bridge: FHWA forecasts domestic water-mode freight tonnage, not constant-price demand for 488320 services, at 0.8% annual growth from 2017 to 2050. I compound that modal rate over five years as the base anchor and widen for international trade, vessel and call patterns, cargo mix, regional routing, and changes in handling intensity. The proxy may understate import/export container handling, which is not fully represented by domestic water mode, and may overstate handlers exposed to declining coal or petroleum cargoes.
d5.rationale: Marine cargo handling remains tied to physical cargo flows. The federal modal forecast supports modest growth, and more than 300 US waterside ports handled $2.28 trillion of international trade in 2022. Demand can be resilient where facilities are strategic and customers require specialist handling, but commodity cycles, tariffs, route changes, port competition, strikes, weather, and automation-driven throughput changes create wide local outcomes.
d5.change_evidence: Extract FAF 5.7.1 waterborne and international-gateway forecasts by target port and commodity, then combine them with contracted minimums, vessel-call schedules, customer volume plans, port capacity projects, and historical handling volumes.
caveats: FAF5.2 forecasts embed assumptions available in 2021 and precede later trade-policy and routing changes.
Tonnage does not map one-for-one to labor or revenue: containers, vehicles, dry bulk, liquid bulk, break-bulk, and project cargo have different handling intensity.
National growth can coexist with severe declines at a single port, terminal, commodity, or customer.

o.bridge: Census defines the industry as physical stevedoring and marine cargo handling, while GAO observes both process and equipment automation at ports. These sources do not measure the five-year operator-required share. I infer that software and autonomous equipment can remove individual tasks and some staffing, but an accountable handling operator remains necessary to plan, supervise, maintain equipment, manage exceptions, meet safety and security duties, and bear performance liability. The estimate may be high if vertically integrated carriers self-handle more cargo or if highly autonomous terminals spread rapidly.
o.rationale: Cargo still must move between vessel and shore even when gates, cranes, and yard vehicles are automated. Only four of the ten largest US container ports in GAO's review used automated cargo-handling equipment, and performance effects were mixed. The ILA-USMX agreement further points to modernization within an organized employer-worker operating framework rather than elimination of the operator. Operator persistence is somewhat lower than in pure infrastructure maintenance because customers can integrate handling or consolidate terminal networks.
o.change_evidence: Track fully autonomous handling deployments, labor per move, remote-control ratios, carrier self-handling, contract insourcing, terminal consolidation, safety incidents, and regulatory or labor-agreement changes across US cargo types.
caveats: Container-terminal automation is not representative of all bulk, break-bulk, vehicle, and project-cargo work.
The distinction between a cargo-handling operator and an integrated port or carrier can change without eliminating physical handling.
This primitive is separate from implemented labor opportunity in rho and demand quantity in d5.

narrative.executive_view: Marine cargo handling has a high labor burden but a smaller AI-substitutable share than the headline labor intensity suggests because much of the payroll is physical, safety-sensitive work. AI can improve manifests, planning, dispatch, yard sequencing, billing, maintenance, safety reporting, and exception management. Existing port automation proves the direction, while labor agreements, capital needs, and mixed equipment performance slow implementation. The industry is transferable and consolidating, but transparent per-unit and cost-plus pricing limits retention.
narrative.ai_impact: The near-term opportunity is to surround longshore and equipment work with better decisions: parse documents, forecast labor and equipment demand, assign moves, optimize sequences, predict equipment failures, reconcile counts, draft reports, and automate routine customer communication. Process automation is already common at large ports, so a must target remaining manual exceptions and fragmented back offices. Autonomous handling equipment can reduce direct labor, but its economics require scale, site redesign, integration, and negotiated work rules.
narrative.value_capture: Handlers can keep some gains within a contract term and can sell better throughput, turnaround, visibility, safety, and reliability. However, rates often expose units, hours, or costs, and concentrated carriers, terminals, and cargo owners can demand repricing. Minimum quantities soften volume risk without necessarily protecting margin. Union wage and benefit commitments can also absorb part of the gain, making capacity expansion and avoided hiring more realistic capture mechanisms than layoffs.
narrative.firm_availability: The six-digit definition closely matches outsourced service providers, but some operations are captive or embedded in large terminal and logistics groups. Recent acquisitions by Pangaea, AMPORTS, and Enstructure show active strategic consolidation and the transferability of handling operations. The public examples are larger than the LMM lens, and local operating rights, union obligations, equipment, and customer relationships must transfer together.
narrative.demand_durability: Cargo flows create unavoidable physical handling demand, and the national water-freight outlook supports modest growth. The operator remains necessary even in an automated terminal. Durability is target-specific: a diversified handler at a strategic port differs radically from a single-customer, single-commodity stevedore. Trade policy, commodity cycles, route changes, strikes, weather, port capital projects, and carrier consolidation are principal demand risks.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest evidence gap is six-digit task composition. Available BLS data cover broader water-support activities, while GAO automation research emphasizes major container ports. LMM bulk and break-bulk handlers may have different economics. Contract pass-through, local labor rules, concession transfer, customer concentration, equipment condition, safety record, and environmental liabilities require target-level diligence. The 2030 East/Gulf labor agreement materially limits five-year freedom to redesign work.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can improve a costly planning, documentation, and equipment-utilization layer around recurring physical cargo moves, and recent consolidation demonstrates transferable operations.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor remains physical and labor-protected, while per-unit and cost-plus contracting gives powerful customers a clear path to recapture productivity gains.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: Defines NAICS 488320 as establishments primarily providing stevedoring and other marine cargo handling services except warehousing and distinguishes packing, warehousing, and port-operation activities.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 488300
sources.supports: Broader water-support occupation mix: 95,160 jobs; 61.16% transportation/material moving; 27.71% material movers; 17.10% hand laborers; 14.06% installation, maintenance and repair; and 10.76% office/administrative support.

sources.title: Port Infrastructure: U.S. Ports Have Adopted Some Automation Technologies and Report Varied Effects
sources.supports: GAO found process automation at terminals in all ten largest US container ports, automated cargo-handling equipment at four, and mixed effects involving performance, workforce, security, cost, and labor agreements.

sources.title: ILA and USMX Sign Six-Year Master Contract Agreement
sources.supports: Documents the October 2024-September 2030 East and Gulf Coast agreement, its current-job protection and technology framework, labor peace, and the ILA president's description of automation protection and a 62% wage increase.

sources.title: Port Performance Freight Statistics Program: Annual Report to Congress 2018
sources.supports: Distinguishes private terminals owned by vessel operators, cargo interests, and independent marine terminal operators, and explains varied ownership, revenue, control, and operating structures.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: 2019 ABS infographic for reference year 2018 reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older, 43% were 35-54, and 6% were 34 or younger.

sources.title: Pangaea Logistics Solutions Announces Completion of Port and Terminal Operations Acquisition
sources.supports: Documents Pangaea's June 2023 acquisition of marine port terminal operations in Florida and Maryland from Host Terminals and describes dry-bulk cargo-handling services.

sources.title: AMPORTS Acquires Red Hook ConRo Terminal at Port Freeport
sources.supports: Documents a 2026 acquisition of an operating stevedoring and terminal business and AMPORTS' expansion of active US stevedoring operations.

sources.title: Enstructure to Acquire LOGISTEC Marine Terminal Division
sources.supports: Documents a June 2026 agreement to acquire LOGISTEC's North American marine terminal operations, including bulk, break-bulk, and container cargo-handling services across 62 ports and 84 terminals.

sources.title: Pangaea Logistics Solutions Ltd. 2024 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes stevedoring contracts priced per unit with possible minimum quantities and expected-cost-plus-margin allocation, terminal service contracts, and direct labor and equipment-related terminal expenses.

sources.title: Freight Analysis Framework Version 5
sources.supports: FAF5.2 baseline forecast shows domestic water-mode freight tonnage increasing from 662.453 million tons in 2017 to 855.079 million in 2050, a 0.8% compound annual growth rate, with wide commodity variation.

sources.title: U.S. Port Infrastructure: DOT and DHS Offer Funding and Other Assistance Ports Can Use to Improve Disaster Resilience
sources.supports: Reports that more than 300 US waterside ports handled over $2.28 trillion of international trade in 2022 and describes the physical coordination and resilience role of port and terminal operators.
